chunk_summary: Management of pneumonia with hypoxia in SAM involves aggressive treatment, oxygen therapy, empiric antibiotics, and step-down to oral agents once stable, following local guidelines and clinical reassessment.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs and diagnostic criteria for severe pneumonia in SAM, including hypoxia and cyanosis.
Learn the first-line empiric antibiotic regimens for pneumonia in SAM and indications for anti-staphylococcal coverage.
Recognize step-down criteria for transitioning from injectable to oral antibiotics in pneumonia management.
Identify investigations that guide management, such as blood cultures, chest radiographs, and electrolyte assessments.
key_facts: Hypoxia in SAM pneumonia is defined as oxygen saturation <90% (<94% with pneumonia).
First-line empiric antibiotics: injectable ampicillin plus gentamicin; add cloxacillin for suspected staphylococcal pneumonia.
Step-down to oral antibiotics when respiratory distress resolves, oxygen requirement stops, and oral feeds are tolerated.
Complete antibiotic course: 5–7 days for uncomplicated pneumonia; longer for staphylococcal pneumonia or complications.
Investigations: blood culture before antibiotics, chest radiograph for effusion or pneumatocele, electrolytes for dehydration or shock.
reference_quote: Empiric antibiotics for admitted/complicated SAM should align to one local standard. A common first-line regimen is injectable ampicillin plus gentamicin. Add anti-staphylococcal coverage (e.g., cloxacillin) when staphylococcal pneumonia is suspected.
